paragraphs.text: When men did not repent — though Noah by his holiness served as a model for his contemporaries, and by his righteousness for a hundred whole years preached to them about the Flood, and they even mocked Noah when he announced to them that all kinds of living creatures would come to him to seek salvation in the ark, and they said, “How shall the beasts and birds come, scattered as they are throughout all lands?” — then God repeated again to him: “Come thou and all thy house into the ark; for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation. Of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens, the male and his female: and of beasts that are not clean by two, the male and his female” (Gen 7:1–2). Clean beasts are gentle animals; unclean are harmful ones. And from the very beginning God created clean animals in larger number.

And behold, those whom the word did not convince had to be convinced by what they saw. “For yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain upon the earth forty days and forty nights; and I will destroy… every thing that I have made” (Gen 7:4). On that very day elephants began to come from the east, monkeys and peacocks from the south, other animals were gathering from the west, others hurried from the north. Lions left their thickets, fierce beasts came out of their lairs, deer and wild asses came from their deserts, animals that lived in the mountains gathered from there. Noah’s contemporaries flocked together at such a new spectacle — but not for repentance, only to take pleasure in seeing how before their eyes lions entered the ark, oxen hurried after them without fear seeking refuge with them, wolves and sheep entered together, hawks and sparrows, eagles and doves.

When even this swift gathering of the beasts into the ark, and the peace that quickly settled between them, did not move Noah’s contemporaries to repentance, then the Lord God said to Noah: “Yet seven days, and I will destroy every flesh I have made.” God gave men a hundred years for repentance while the ark was being built, but they did not come to their senses. He gathered the beasts they had never seen before — yet men would not repent; He established peace between harmful and harmless animals — yet they were not afraid. Even after Noah and all the animals had entered the ark, God delayed yet seven days, leaving the door of the ark open. It is just as wonderful that neither did the lions remember their forests nor the other beasts and birds of every kind seek again their dwellings, as that Noah’s contemporaries, seeing all that took place outside and within the ark, were not convinced to leave their ungodly deeds.

God wished to delay punishment of the wicked people for a hundred and twenty years, first, that they might repent; further, that during this time the righteous who lived among them might become their judges; finally, that the righteous might fulfill the years of their life, and there be no cause to say: “Why did God not deliver from destruction those who did not sin?” Testing the human race for a hundred years, God then reduced it by twenty years, because those seven days during which He delayed after the entry of the animals into the ark, by the signs accomplished then, were of more weight than the twenty years subtracted. For if at the signs accomplished during those seven days men did not repent, it is plain they would not have repented during the twenty years that passed without signs. Therefore the One who took away the twenty years thereby spared men a greater number of transgressions.

When the seven days were fulfilled, “in the six hundredth year of Noah’s life, in the second month, the twenty-seventh day of the month… were all the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened… and the Lord God shut him in” (Gen 7:11, 16) — so that, when the waters rose, no one could break in the door of the ark and enter it. “And the flood was… And every flesh died… and Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark” (Gen 7:17, 21, 23). The fountains of the deep and the windows of heaven were open for forty days and nights, and the ark floated upon the waves for a hundred and fifty days.
